Project Foundations for Data Science: FoodHub Data Analysis¶

Marks: 60

Context¶

The number of restaurants in New York is increasing day by day. Lots of students and busy professionals rely on those restaurants due to their hectic lifestyles. Online food delivery service is a great option for them. It provides them with good food from their favorite restaurants. A food aggregator company FoodHub offers access to multiple restaurants through a single smartphone app.

The app allows the restaurants to receive a direct online order from a customer. The app assigns a delivery person from the company to pick up the order after it is confirmed by the restaurant. The delivery person then uses the map to reach the restaurant and waits for the food package. Once the food package is handed over to the delivery person, he/she confirms the pick-up in the app and travels to the customer's location to deliver the food. The delivery person confirms the drop-off in the app after delivering the food package to the customer. The customer can rate the order in the app. The food aggregator earns money by collecting a fixed margin of the delivery order from the restaurants.

Objective¶

The food aggregator company has stored the data of the different orders made by the registered customers in their online portal. They want to analyze the data to get a fair idea about the demand of different restaurants which will help them in enhancing their customer experience. Suppose you are hired as a Data Scientist in this company and the Data Science team has shared some of the key questions that need to be answered. Perform the data analysis to find answers to these questions that will help the company to improve the business.

Data Description¶

The data contains the different data related to a food order. The detailed data dictionary is given below.

Data Dictionary¶

  • order_id: Unique ID of the order
  • customer_id: ID of the customer who ordered the food
  • restaurant_name: Name of the restaurant
  • cuisine_type: Cuisine ordered by the customer
  • cost: Cost of the order
  • day_of_the_week: Indicates whether the order is placed on a weekday or weekend (The weekday is from Monday to Friday and the weekend is Saturday and Sunday)
  • rating: Rating given by the customer out of 5
  • food_preparation_time: Time (in minutes) taken by the restaurant to prepare the food. This is calculated by taking the difference between the timestamps of the restaurant's order confirmation and the delivery person's pick-up confirmation.
  • delivery_time: Time (in minutes) taken by the delivery person to deliver the food package. This is calculated by taking the difference between the timestamps of the delivery person's pick-up confirmation and drop-off information

Let us start by importing the required libraries¶

In [103]:
# import libraries for data manipulation
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

# import libraries for data visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Command to tell Python to actually display the graphs
%matplotlib inline

# to restrict the float value to 3 decimal places
pd.set_option('display.float_format', lambda x: '%.3f' % x)

# Library to suppress warnings
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

  5. Column cost_of_the_order is of type float

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)¶

Univariate Analysis¶

Question 6: Explore all the variables and provide observations on their distributions. (Generally, histograms, boxplots, countplots, etc. are used for univariate exploration.) [9 marks]¶

In [113]:
#Define method to plot countplot along with percent values for non-continuous data columns
def count_plot_withPercent(data, columnForAnalysis):
  plt.figure(figsize=(15,5))

  data[columnForAnalysis] = data[columnForAnalysis].astype('category')

  ax = sns.countplot(data=data, x = columnForAnalysis, order=data[columnForAnalysis].value_counts().index)

  for bar in ax.patches:
    percentage = '{:.1f}%'.format(bar.get_height()/len(data[columnForAnalysis]) * 100)
    x = bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2 - 0.25
    y = bar.get_y() + bar.get_height()
    ax.annotate(percentage, (x,y), size=12)

  plt.xticks(rotation = 90)
  plt.show()

  #Print counts of reach unique value
  category = [columnForAnalysis]
  for col in category:
    print(df[col].value_counts())

  print("There are",data[columnForAnalysis].nunique(),"unique categories in column",columnForAnalysis)

In [120]:
#Define a method to plot a histogram for a given column showing the mean
def plot_histogramNBoxPlot(data, columnName, figsize=(15,10)):
  ax = sns.histplot(data=data,x=columnName,kde=True)
  mean_value = np.mean(df[columnName])
  median_value = np.median(df[columnName])
  ax.axvline(mean_value,color='g',linestyle='--',label='Mean')
  ax.axvline(median_value,color='b',linestyle='-', label='Median')

  plt.show()

  ay = sns.boxplot(data=data,x=columnName, showmeans=True)
  # Calculate statistics and annotate
  stats = data[columnName].describe()
  plt.show()
  print(stats)

25%       20.000

Observation¶

  1. Food delivery time shows a well centered distribution. The mean is 24.16 while the median is 25. The mean and median values are close.
  2. The mean is slightly less than the median suggesting that orders that are delivered quickly are skewing the mean slightly to the left.
  3. The shortest delivery takes 15 minutes while the longest takes 33 minutes.

1200 rows × 1 columns


In [129]:
def DetermineFirstN(data, category, N):
  categoryArray = [category]
  count = 0

  for columnCategory in categoryArray:
      resultValueCounts = data[columnCategory].value_counts()
      while(count < N):
        print(count, "customer with ID",resultValueCounts.index[count],"has made", resultValueCounts.values[count], "orders.")
        count +=1

DetermineFirstN(df,'customer_id',3)

0 customer with ID 52832 has made 13 orders.
1 customer with ID 47440 has made 10 orders.
2 customer with ID 83287 has made 9 orders.

Observations:¶

  1. The top 3 customers that qualify for 20% discount are:
  • customer with ID 52832 has made 13 orders.
  • customer with ID 47440 has made 10 orders.
  • customer with ID 83287 has made 9 orders.

Multivariate Analysis¶

Question 12: Perform a multivariate analysis to explore relationships between the important variables in the dataset. (It is a good idea to explore relations between numerical variables as well as relations between numerical and categorical variables) [10 marks]¶

In [130]:
#Create method to create a heatmap of correlations between numeric data types
def createHeatMap(data):
  numeric_columns = data.select_dtypes(include=['number'])
  sns.heatmap(numeric_columns.corr(),annot=True,cmap='coolwarm')
In [131]:
#create heatmap of numeric data types in df dataset
createHeatMap(df)

Observations¶

  1. There is a small positive correlation between food preparation time and cost of the order. Foods that take longer to prepare cost more. The correlation is 0.042.
  2. There is also a small positive correlation (0.011) between food preparation time and delivery time.
  3. There is a small negative correlation (-0.03) between cost of the order and delivery time. Less costly orders get delivered slightly faster.
  4. In general, there are no strong correlations between the numeric variables
In [132]:
#Method to create a box plot between two variables
def createBoxPlot(data,x,y):
  plt.figure(figsize=(12,7))
  order = data.groupby(x)[y].median().sort_values(ascending=False).index
  sns.boxplot(data,x=x,y=y, order=order, palette='Set1',showmeans=True)
  plt.xticks(rotation = 90)
In [133]:
createBoxPlot(df,'cuisine_type','cost_of_the_order')

Observations¶

  1. 50% of French orders cost 20 dollars or more. French cuisine has the highest median.
  2. Vietnamese food has the lowest median of 13 dollars.
  3. Mediterranean food shows outliers where some orders cost a lot. Korean food shows outliers on the lower and upper price range
  4. Thai food is well distributed around the median price. The mean and median have the same value
In [134]:
#Examine relationship between cuisine and food preparation time
createBoxPlot(df,'cuisine_type','food_preparation_time')

Observations¶

  1. 50 percent of italian orders take 28 minutes or more to prepare
  2. In general, the median of all the cuisine preparation time falls between about 25 - 28 minutes.
  3. 50 percent of Vietnamese cuisine orders take the shortest time to prepare of about 25 minutes
  4. There are outliers in the preparation time for Korean food, where some orders take a long time to prepare
In [135]:
# Examine relationship between the day of the week and delivery time
createBoxPlot(df,'day_of_the_week','delivery_time')

Observations:¶

  1. Orders take longer to deliver during the weekday (median is about 28 minutes) that during the weekend (median is about 22 minutes). It is possible that there is less traffic during the weekend or perhaps more delivery drivers during the weekend.
In [136]:
#Explore the ralationship between the rating and the cost of the order
def createPointPlot(data,x,y):
  plt.figure(figsize=(12,7))
  sns.pointplot(data,x=x,y=y)
createPointPlot(df,'rating','cost_of_the_order')
createBoxPlot(df,'rating','cost_of_the_order')

Observations¶

  1. Orders that get a high rating of 5 tend to be more costly. 50% of orders that cost 15 dollars or more get a rating of 5.
  2. As the cost of the order decreases, the rating decreases (which is unexpected).
In [137]:
#Explore relationship between rating and food preparation time
createPointPlot(df,'rating','food_preparation_time')
createBoxPlot(df,'rating','food_preparation_time')

Observations¶

  1. The food preparation time does not change much for the various ratings assigned by customers
  2. Food preparation time does not seem correlated with rating
In [138]:
#Explore the relationship between rating and delivery time
createPointPlot(df,'rating','delivery_time')
createBoxPlot(df,'rating','delivery_time')

Observations¶

  1. Shorter delivery times lead to better ratings e.g. 50% of the orders delivered in 24 minutes or less get a rating of 4. For the lowest rating of 3, 50% if delivery times took 25 minutes or more.
  2. However, 50% of ratings with the highest score of 5 took 25 minutes or more for delivery, suggesting that the meal itself may influence ratings stronger

In [140]:
df['Revenue'] = df.groupby('restaurant_name')['cost_of_the_order'].transform('sum')
df['OrderCount'] = df.groupby('restaurant_name')['order_id'].transform('count')
In [141]:
#Understand how order counts relate to revenue
def createScatterPlot(data,x,y,z):
  plt.figure(figsize=(12,7))
  sns.scatterplot(data=data,x=x,y=y,hue=z,palette='Set1')

createScatterPlot(df,'OrderCount','Revenue','cuisine_type')

Observations¶

  1. To increase revenue a restuarant should strive to get more orders
  2. An American cuisine restuarant has the highest number of orders and the highest revenue. The difference of the restuarant with the highest revenue to other restuarants is significant.
In [142]:
def createPairPlot(data):
  plt.figure(figsize=(15,7))
  sns.pairplot(data=data)
  plt.show()

createPairPlot(df[['cost_of_the_order','delivery_time','OrderCount','Revenue','food_preparation_time']])

<Figure size 1500x700 with 0 Axes>

Observations¶

  1. The strongest correlation is between order count and revenue. The higher the number of orders, the higher the revenue
  2. From the histogram of revenue and order count, we see that a lot of restuarants make little money and have few orders
  3. For many other variables, it is challenging to determine the correlations
In [143]:
sns.countplot(df,x='day_of_the_week',hue='rating')
Out[143]:
<Axes: xlabel='day_of_the_week', ylabel='count'>

Observations¶

  1. There are more orders over the weekend
  2. The ratings follow the same trend whether an order is placed during a weekday or weekend. Most orders do not receive a rating. The highest frequency ratiing is 5 followed by 4 and then 3.
In [144]:
df_crosstab = pd.crosstab(df['day_of_the_week'],df['rating'])
df_crosstab.plot(kind='bar', stacked=True)
Out[144]:
<Axes: xlabel='day_of_the_week'>

Observations¶

  1. There are more orders over the weekend
  2. The ratings follow the same trend whether an order is placed during a weekday or weekend. Most orders do not receive a rating. The highest frequency ratiing is 5 followed by 4 and then 3.

Question 13: The company wants to provide a promotional offer in the advertisement of the restaurants. The condition to get the offer is that the restaurants must have a rating count of more than 50 and the average rating should be greater than 4. Find the restaurants fulfilling the criteria to get the promotional offer. [3 marks]¶

In [145]:
#Create a dataframe with restuarants where rating is assigned and convert it to numeric type
dfwithRatingAssigned = df[df['rating']!='Not given']
dfwithRatingAssigned['rating'] = dfwithRatingAssigned['rating'].astype(str)
dfwithRatingAssigned['rating_numeric'] = pd.to_numeric(dfwithRatingAssigned['rating'])

#Create new columns with the count of ratings and rating average
dfwithRatingAssigned['rating_count'] = dfwithRatingAssigned.groupby('restaurant_name')['rating_numeric'].transform('count')
dfwithRatingAssigned['rating_average'] = dfwithRatingAssigned.groupby('restaurant_name')['rating_numeric'].transform('mean')

#Find restaurants with rating count of > 50 and an average rating >4
dfQualifyForAdvertPromotion = dfwithRatingAssigned.loc[(dfwithRatingAssigned['rating_count']>50) & (dfwithRatingAssigned['rating_average']>4)]
dfQualifyForAdvertPromotion.shape

#Restaurants qualifying for the promotional advertisement are:
category = ['restaurant_name']
for cat in category:
  count = dfQualifyForAdvertPromotion[cat].value_counts(dropna=True)
  count = count[count>0]
  print(count)

restaurant_name
Shake Shack                  133
The Meatball Shop             84
Blue Ribbon Sushi             73
Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken     64
Name: count, dtype: int64

Observation¶

  1. The restuarants qualifying for the promotion are: Shake Shack, The Meatball Shop, Blue Ribbon Sushi and Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken

Question 14: The company charges the restaurant 25% on the orders having cost greater than 20 dollars and 15% on the orders having cost greater than 5 dollars. Find the net revenue generated by the company across all orders. [3 marks]¶

In [146]:
def determineFoodDeliveryCharge(order_cost):
  if(order_cost > 20):
    return order_cost*0.25
  elif(5 < order_cost <= 20): #Noticed that the minimum food cost is 4.470 but decided to treat it like $5 since it is reasonable that the food delivery company should change a fee even for low cost orders
    return order_cost*0.15
  else:
    return 0

#Create a new column is dataframe with the delivery charge:
df['Delivery_Fee'] = df['cost_of_the_order'].apply(determineFoodDeliveryCharge)

print('The delivery service total revenue is',df['Delivery_Fee'].sum(),'dollars')

The delivery service total revenue is 6166.303 dollars

Observations:¶

The delivery service total revenue is 6172.714 dollars

Question 15: The company wants to analyze the total time required to deliver the food. What percentage of orders take more than 60 minutes to get delivered from the time the order is placed? (The food has to be prepared and then delivered.) [2 marks]¶

In [147]:
#Compute the total food delivery time which is the sum of preparation and delivery time
df['Total_Food_Delivery_Time'] = df['food_preparation_time'] + df['delivery_time']

print(len(df[df['Total_Food_Delivery_Time']>60]))

percentOrdersWithGreaterThan60MinutesForDelivery = round(len(df[df['Total_Food_Delivery_Time']>60])/len(df['Total_Food_Delivery_Time']) * 100,2)
print('Percentage of orders that take more than 60 minutes to deliver is',percentOrdersWithGreaterThan60MinutesForDelivery,'%')

200
Percentage of orders that take more than 60 minutes to deliver is 10.54 %

Observations:¶

Question 16: The company wants to analyze the delivery time of the orders on weekdays and weekends. How does the mean delivery time vary during weekdays and weekends? [2 marks]¶

In [148]:
#Method to detemine mean delivery time for different categories in a dataframe column
def determineMeanForCategory(data, columnName, categoryColumn):
  category = [categoryColumn]
  for cat in category:
    meanForCategory = data.groupby(cat)[columnName].mean()
    print(meanForCategory)

determineMeanForCategory(df,'delivery_time','day_of_the_week')
determineMeanForCategory(df,'Total_Food_Delivery_Time','day_of_the_week')

day_of_the_week
Weekday   28.340
Weekend   22.470
Name: delivery_time, dtype: float64
day_of_the_week
Weekday   55.552
Weekend   49.907
Name: Total_Food_Delivery_Time, dtype: float64

Observations:¶

  1. On average, the delivery times over the weekend is shorter (22.47 minutes) compared to during the weekday (28.34 minutes)
  2. Similarly, the average total delivery time (preparation + delivery) is shorter during the weekend (49.9 minutes) compared to the weekdays (55.6 minutes)

Conclusion and Recommendations¶

Question 17: What are your conclusions from the analysis? What recommendations would you like to share to help improve the business? (You can use cuisine type and feedback ratings to drive your business recommendations.) [6 marks]¶

In [149]:

#Create a heatmap of the above data
plt.figure(figsize=(15,10))
sns.heatmap(data = ct,cmap='coolwarm',annot=True)
plt.show()

Conclusions:¶

In this project, we reviewed data from a food aggregator company Foodhub that offers access to several restaurants through a smartphone application. The company has delivery staff that picks up the orders and delivers it to customers. We gain the following insights from the data.

  1. The data covers 1898 orders from 178 restaurants supported by the company and providing 14 unique cuisine types.
  2. Most orders do not receive a rating (38.9% of the orders are not given a rating).
  3. American cuisine orders are most popular followed by Japanese. Vietnamese food has the least number of orders.
  4. Most orders are placed on the weekends rather than on the weekdays
  5. Shake shack is the restuarant with the highest number of orders and receives the highest revenue.
  6. There is a small positive correlation between the cost of the order and the food preparation time. More costly orders take a long time to prepare.
  7. French food has the highest median cost of slightly greater than 20 dollars. Although Vietnamese food has the lowest median cost, it also had the least number of total orders. A lower cost of the order does not necesarily lead to more orders which is unexpected.
  8. Delivery times are significantly shorter during the weekends (about 22 minutes median) compared to weekdays (about 28 minute median).
  9. More costly orders receive higher ratings, orders with rating of 5 have the highest average cost per order. This is unexpected.
  10. Faster delivery times can improve ratings. The lowest average delivery time has a rating of 4.
  11. The food hub company is making the most money from American cuisine restaurants. The company could prioritize supporting more American cuisine restairants
  12. Restuarants with very positive ratings (5) also allow the company to generate more income. The company can encourage restaurants to improve their ratings since this leads to more orders and more delivery fees for the food hub company.

Recommendations:¶

  1. Prioritize supporting more American and Japanese restaurants for profitability
  2. Bring in high rating restaurants into the food hub support network to improve profitability. Restaurants with high ratings receive more orders.
  3. Encourage restaurants to improve their ratings in order to increase customer orders and to generate higher income for the food hub company
  4. Faster delivery times can improve ratings and higher ratings lead to more orders. The company should focus on reducing delivery times.
